input.requested_service: MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553)

source_quotes.quote: Your request for an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) has been DENIED.

Reason for denial: Request does not meet medical necessity criteria for advanced imaging in the evaluation of headache per Northstar Imaging Policy IMG-HEAD-v7. The submitted documentation does not demonstrate presence of red-flag features or failure of first-line management sufficient to justify advanced imaging at this time.
Denial code: IMG-N-04 (insufficient clinical red flags documented).
You have the right to appeal. Appeals must reference the specific red-flag criteria in policy IMG-HEAD-v7 and include supporting clinical documentation.

citations.guideline_source: American College of Radiology (ACR) 2019 Appropriateness Criteria - Headache
citations.quote: MRI of the head without and with IV contrast is usually appropriate for patients with chronic headache showing new features, increasing frequency, or a change from baseline pattern, and for headache with neurologic deficit or other red-flag features.
citations.supports_claim: Advanced brain MRI is appropriate when a patient has a significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity, or new neurologic features.

citations.citation_title: Evidence-Based Guideline Update: Pharmacologic Treatment for Episodic Migraine Prevention in Adults
citations.guideline_source: American Academy of Neurology (AAN) and American Headache Society (AHS) 2012 (reaffirmed)
citations.quote: Neuroimaging should be considered in patients with non-acute headache and an unexplained abnormal neurologic examination finding, or with atypical headache features or headaches that do not fulfill the strict definition of migraine.
citations.supports_claim: Neuroimaging is indicated when headache features are atypical or accompanied by neurologic findings, supporting medical necessity of brain MRI.

citations.citation_title: Practice Parameter: The Utility of Neuroimaging in the Evaluation of Headache in Patients with Normal Neurologic Examinations
citations.quote: Neuroimaging should be considered in patients with headaches that have changed in pattern, are not consistent with migraine, or are associated with focal neurologic signs or symptoms, papilledema, or other red-flag features.
citations.supports_claim: Brain imaging is medically necessary for patients with a change in headache pattern or red-flag features such as focal deficits or papilledema.

citations.claim: The service denied is a brain MRI with and without contrast (CPT 70553).
citations.verbatim_quote: Your request for an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) has been DENIED.

citations.claim: The denial was issued under policy IMG-HEAD-v7 on grounds of allegedly absent red-flag features or failure of first-line therapy.
citations.verbatim_quote: The submitted documentation does not demonstrate presence of red-flag features or failure of first-line management sufficient to justify advanced imaging at this time.

citations.claim: The assigned denial code is IMG-N-04.
citations.verbatim_quote: Denial code: IMG-N-04 (insufficient clinical red flags documented).

citations.claim: The plan invites appeals referencing the specific red-flag criteria of IMG-HEAD-v7.
citations.verbatim_quote: Appeals must reference the specific red-flag criteria in policy IMG-HEAD-v7 and include supporting clinical documentation.

paragraphs.heading: Patient and Requested Service
paragraphs.text: This letter formally appeals Northstar Gold PPO's March 28, 2026 denial of prior authorization for Robert K. Lee (member ID NS-77881266, DOB October 3, 1984). The service at issue is an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553), ordered by the treating neurologist, Dr. Patel. The denial letter states that the request has been denied, citing denial code IMG-N-04 for allegedly insufficient red-flag documentation under Northstar Imaging Policy IMG-HEAD-v7. As permitted by the appeal rights set forth in the denial letter, this appeal references the specific red-flag criteria in IMG-HEAD-v7 and provides the supporting clinical documentation demonstrating that multiple criteria are, in fact, met.

citations.claim: Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 requires only one red-flag criterion to establish medical necessity.
citations.verbatim_quote: Advanced neuroimaging is considered medically necessary when at least ONE of the following red-flag criteria is documented in the medical record:

citations.claim: Thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute is a qualifying red-flag criterion.
citations.verbatim_quote: (a) Sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute.

citations.claim: The patient presented with sudden worst headache of life during exertion.
citations.verbatim_quote: Chief complaint: sudden worst headache of life while exercising, onset 30 minutes prior.

citations.claim: The treating neurologist explicitly documented a new-onset thunderclap headache.
citations.verbatim_quote: New-onset "thunderclap" headache with transient focal neurologic deficit is a significant change from his established migraine pattern.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Thunderclap headache (criterion mn_1)
paragraphs.text: Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 establishes that advanced neuroimaging is medically necessary when at least ONE red-flag criterion is documented, and criterion (a) is sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute. Mr. Lee's presentation meets this criterion unambiguously. At the Urgent Care visit of March 5, 2026, he presented with the sudden worst headache of his life while exercising, with onset only 30 minutes prior. His treating neurologist subsequently characterized the event as a new-onset "thunderclap" headache representing a significant change from his established migraine pattern. This satisfies criterion (a) on its own and is consistent with the ACR Appropriateness Criteria: Headache (2019), which identifies thunderclap presentations as appropriate indications for brain MRI.

citations.claim: A new or progressive focal neurologic deficit associated with headache is a qualifying red flag.
citations.verbatim_quote: (b) New or progressive focal neurologic deficit (motor, sensory, visual, speech) associated with the headache.

citations.claim: The first episode included focal sensory deficits documented on exam.
citations.verbatim_quote: Associated with brief (90-second) left-hand paresthesia. BP 148/92. Neuro exam: transiently diminished proprioception in left hand, resolved during exam.

citations.claim: A second episode involved a focal visual deficit.
citations.verbatim_quote: Second similar episode 2026-03-09 with right-sided visual blur lasting 2 minutes.

citations.claim: A third episode involved a focal motor deficit (facial droop).
citations.verbatim_quote: Third episode of transient focal symptoms (right-sided facial droop, 3 minutes) overnight 2026-03-20.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: New focal neurologic deficits (criterion mn_2)
paragraphs.text: Policy criterion (b) is satisfied by a new or progressive focal neurologic deficit — motor, sensory, visual, or speech — associated with the headache. Mr. Lee's chart documents all three modalities across three separate episodes. On March 5, 2026 he had a 90-second left-hand paresthesia with objectively diminished proprioception on neurologic exam (sensory deficit). On March 9, 2026 he experienced a right-sided visual blur lasting two minutes (visual deficit). On March 20, 2026 he had a three-minute right-sided facial droop (motor deficit). These objective, examiner-witnessed and patient-reported focal findings are precisely the red-flag features the policy enumerates, and they are the type of findings that the AAN/AHS and ACR guidelines identify as indications for neuroimaging.

citations.claim: A significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity is a qualifying red-flag criterion.
citations.verbatim_quote: (c) Significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline.

citations.claim: The neurologist documented that the new features are a significant change from baseline and are new red-flag features.
citations.verbatim_quote: These are NEW red-flag features not previously present.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Significant change from established baseline (criterion mn_3)
paragraphs.text: Policy criterion (c) is met when there is a significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline. Mr. Lee has a well-documented history of established migraine under Dr. Patel's care. His treating neurologist explicitly concluded in the March 12, 2026 progress note that the new thunderclap presentation and the transient focal neurologic deficits are NEW red-flag features not previously present and represent a significant change from his established migraine pattern. This is exactly the clinical scenario criterion (c) was written to cover, and it aligns with the AAN 2000 Practice Parameter (reaffirmed) recommendation that neuroimaging be considered when a patient's headaches change in pattern or are associated with focal neurologic signs.

citations.claim: Failure of at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine satisfies the first-line-therapy red flag.
citations.verbatim_quote: (f) Failure of adequate trial of first-line therapy (including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine).

citations.claim: Topiramate, the first preventive, produced only partial benefit with continued breakthrough migraines.
citations.verbatim_quote: Established migraine. On topiramate 50mg BID with partial benefit. Continues 2-3 migraines per month.

citations.claim: Erenumab, the second preventive, was added but frequency worsened to 4-6 migraines per month.
citations.verbatim_quote: Added erenumab 70mg monthly. Patient reports 4-6 migraines/month now, down from baseline.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Failure of first-line preventive therapy (criterion mn_6)
paragraphs.text: Policy criterion (f) requires failure of an adequate trial of first-line therapy, including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine. Mr. Lee has failed two guideline-recommended preventive agents. First, he was maintained on topiramate 50 mg BID with only partial benefit, continuing to experience 2-3 migraines per month. Second, erenumab 70 mg monthly was added, after which his migraine frequency actually increased to 4-6 per month. Two preventive agents at adequate dose and duration have therefore been tried and failed, independently satisfying criterion (f) and directly rebutting the denial letter's assertion that failure of first-line management was not documented.

paragraphs.heading: Conclusion and Request
paragraphs.text: Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 requires only a single red-flag criterion to establish medical necessity for advanced neuroimaging. Mr. Lee independently satisfies four: thunderclap onset (criterion a), new focal neurologic deficits across sensory, visual, and motor modalities (criterion b), a significant change from his established migraine baseline (criterion c), and failure of two adequate first-line preventive agents (criterion f). His presentation — three discrete episodes of transient focal neurologic symptoms superimposed on a thunderclap onset — raises serious concern for subarachnoid hemorrhage, vascular malformation, or early ischemic disease, the very entities for which MRI of the brain with and without contrast is the appropriate modality and for which CT has known limited sensitivity. We respectfully request that Northstar Gold PPO overturn the IMG-N-04 denial and approve CPT 70553 without further delay. Please direct any questions or requests for additional documentation to the ordering physician's office.

input.subject_line: Appeal of prior authorization denial — MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) — member NS-77881266

citations.citation_title: Evaluation and Treatment of Headache in Adults
citations.guideline_source: American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) 2013 - 'Headache: Diagnosis and Management'
citations.quote: Neuroimaging should be considered in patients with headache who have abnormal neurologic examination findings, atypical headache features, or headaches that do not fulfill strict definitions of migraine or other primary headache disorders, and in those with additional risk factors such as immune deficiency.
citations.supports_claim: Brain MRI is indicated when headache presentation includes red-flag features such as focal neurologic deficits, change in headache pattern, or immunocompromise.

citations.guideline_source: American College of Radiology (ACR) Appropriateness Criteria, Headache (2019 revision)
citations.quote: MRI of the head without and with IV contrast is usually appropriate for patients with chronic headache and new features or a change in clinical presentation, including new neurologic deficit, sudden severe onset, or headache in an immunocompromised or cancer patient.
citations.supports_claim: Advanced neuroimaging (brain MRI) is the appropriate modality when red-flag features or a significant change in headache pattern are present.

citations.citation_title: Practice parameter: The utility of neuroimaging in the evaluation of headache in patients with normal neurologic examinations
citations.quote: Neuroimaging should be considered in patients with non-acute headache and an unexplained abnormal finding on neurologic examination, or in patients with atypical headache features or headaches that do not fulfill the strict definition of migraine.
citations.supports_claim: Neuroimaging is recommended when headache is accompanied by abnormal neurologic findings or atypical/red-flag features.

citations.citation_title: Evaluation of the Adult Patient with Nontraumatic Headache in the Emergency Department
citations.guideline_source: American College of Emergency Physicians (ACEP) Clinical Policy 2019
citations.quote: Patients presenting with sudden-onset severe (thunderclap) headache, new neurologic deficit, or headache with signs of increased intracranial pressure should undergo urgent neuroimaging to exclude secondary causes such as subarachnoid hemorrhage or mass lesion.
citations.supports_claim: Thunderclap headache, focal neurologic deficits, or papilledema warrant urgent advanced neuroimaging.

citations.claim: The plan instructs appeals to reference specific red-flag criteria in IMG-HEAD-v7.
citations.verbatim_quote: Appeals must reference the specific red-flag criteria in policy IMG-HEAD-v7 and include supporting clinical documentation.

paragraphs.heading: Patient and Requested Service
paragraphs.text: I am writing on behalf of my patient, Robert K. Lee (member ID NS-77881266, date of birth October 3, 1984), covered under the Northstar Gold PPO plan, to formally appeal the March 28, 2026 denial of prior authorization for an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553). The denial letter states that the request was denied under denial code IMG-N-04 on the basis that documentation purportedly does not demonstrate red-flag features or failure of first-line management under Northstar Imaging Policy IMG-HEAD-v7. As outlined below, the medical record in fact documents multiple independent red-flag criteria from that policy, and this appeal references the specific red-flag criteria as instructed in the denial letter's appeal-rights paragraph.

citations.claim: Policy requires at least one red-flag criterion to be documented.
citations.verbatim_quote: Advanced neuroimaging is considered medically necessary when at least ONE of the following red-flag criteria is documented in the medical record:

citations.claim: Thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute is a qualifying red flag.
citations.verbatim_quote: Sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute.

citations.claim: Patient presented with sudden worst headache of life during exertion.
citations.verbatim_quote: Chief complaint: sudden worst headache of life while exercising, onset 30 minutes prior.

citations.claim: Treating neurologist characterized the event as a thunderclap headache.
citations.verbatim_quote: New-onset "thunderclap" headache with transient focal neurologic deficit

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Thunderclap headache (criterion a)
paragraphs.text: Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 establishes that advanced neuroimaging is medically necessary when at least ONE red-flag criterion is documented, the first being sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute. Mr. Lee's urgent care presentation on March 5, 2026 meets this criterion precisely: he presented with the "sudden worst headache of life while exercising, onset 30 minutes prior." His treating neurologist subsequently characterized the event explicitly as a "thunderclap" headache. This presentation is exactly the sudden-onset, maximum-intensity headache that national guidance — including the ACEP 2019 Clinical Policy on nontraumatic headache and the ACR Appropriateness Criteria for Headache (2019) — identifies as warranting urgent advanced neuroimaging to exclude secondary causes such as subarachnoid hemorrhage.

citations.claim: Policy lists new/progressive focal motor, sensory, visual, or speech deficit as a red flag.
citations.verbatim_quote: New or progressive focal neurologic deficit (motor, sensory, visual, speech) associated with the headache.

citations.claim: Patient had transient left-hand sensory deficit with objective exam finding.
citations.verbatim_quote: Associated with brief (90-second) left-hand paresthesia. BP 148/92. Neuro exam: transiently diminished proprioception in left hand, resolved during exam.

citations.claim: Patient had a second episode with transient visual deficit.
citations.verbatim_quote: Second similar episode 2026-03-09 with right-sided visual blur lasting 2 minutes.

citations.claim: Patient had a third episode with transient focal motor deficit (facial droop).
citations.verbatim_quote: Third episode of transient focal symptoms (right-sided facial droop, 3 minutes) overnight 2026-03-20.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: New/progressive focal neurologic deficit (criterion b)
paragraphs.text: Policy criterion (b) is satisfied when the headache is accompanied by a new or progressive focal neurologic deficit — motor, sensory, visual, or speech. Mr. Lee has documented focal deficits in all three of the sensory, visual, and motor domains across three separate episodes: (1) on March 5, 2026, a 90-second left-hand paresthesia with objectively diminished proprioception in the left hand on examination; (2) on March 9, 2026, right-sided visual blur lasting two minutes; and (3) overnight on March 20, 2026, a three-minute right-sided facial droop. This constellation of objectively documented, recurrent focal deficits is precisely the presentation for which the AAFP (2013) and AAN practice parameter recommend neuroimaging.

citations.claim: Policy lists significant change from baseline as a red flag.
citations.verbatim_quote: Significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline.

citations.claim: Treating neurologist documents significant change from established migraine pattern.
citations.verbatim_quote: significant change from his established migraine pattern

citations.claim: Red-flag features are newly present and were not part of baseline.
citations.verbatim_quote: These are NEW red-flag features not previously present.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Significant change from established baseline (criterion c)
paragraphs.text: Policy criterion (c) authorizes advanced imaging when there is a significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline. Mr. Lee carries a long-standing migraine diagnosis, and his treating neurologist explicitly documents that the recent thunderclap event with transient focal neurologic deficit represents a significant change from his established migraine pattern, and further that the red-flag features observed are new and were not previously present. This satisfies criterion (c) independently of criteria (a) and (b).

citations.claim: Policy allows imaging on failure of at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine.
citations.verbatim_quote: Failure of adequate trial of first-line therapy (including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine).

citations.claim: Topiramate trial produced only partial benefit with continued migraines.
citations.verbatim_quote: On topiramate 50mg BID with partial benefit. Continues 2-3 migraines per month.

citations.claim: Erenumab added as a second preventive; patient continues 4-6 migraines/month.
citations.verbatim_quote: Added erenumab 70mg monthly. Patient reports 4-6 migraines/month now, down from baseline.

paragraphs.heading: Medical necessity met: Failure of adequate trial of first-line therapy (criterion f)
paragraphs.text: Even setting aside the red flags above, criterion (f) is also satisfied. Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 authorizes advanced imaging on failure of an adequate trial of first-line therapy, including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine. Mr. Lee has had adequate trials of two distinct first-line preventives with inadequate response: (1) topiramate 50 mg BID, on which he continued to experience 2–3 migraines per month with only partial benefit, and (2) erenumab 70 mg monthly, on which he continues to experience 4–6 migraines per month. Both agents are guideline-endorsed first-line migraine preventives, and neither has produced adequate control.

paragraphs.heading: Conclusion and Request
paragraphs.text: Policy IMG-HEAD-v7 requires only ONE red-flag criterion to establish medical necessity for advanced neuroimaging. Mr. Lee independently satisfies FOUR: (a) thunderclap headache, (b) new/progressive focal neurologic deficit across sensory, visual, and motor domains, (c) significant change from his established migraine baseline, and (f) failure of an adequate trial of two first-line preventive agents. This conclusion is consistent with the ACR Appropriateness Criteria — Headache (2019), the ACEP Clinical Policy on nontraumatic headache (2019), the AAN practice parameter on neuroimaging in headache, and the AAFP 2013 guidance on headache evaluation, all of which endorse MRI of the brain in this clinical context. Given the repeated transient focal deficits and thunderclap onset, the diagnostic urgency is real and delay carries risk. I respectfully request that the denial be overturned and that prior authorization for MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) be approved without further delay. Please contact my office if any additional information would be helpful.

criteria.quote: (a) Sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute.
criteria.text: Sudden-onset severe (thunderclap) headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute.

criteria.quote: (b) New or progressive focal neurologic deficit (motor, sensory, visual, speech) associated with the headache.
criteria.text: New or progressive focal neurologic deficit (motor, sensory, visual, or speech) associated with the headache.

criteria.quote: (c) Significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline.
criteria.text: Significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline.

criteria.quote: (d) Headache in an immunocompromised host or in the setting of active malignancy.
criteria.text: Headache occurring in an immunocompromised host or in the setting of active malignancy.

criteria.quote: (e) Papilledema or other signs of increased intracranial pressure.
criteria.text: Papilledema or other signs of increased intracranial pressure.

criteria.quote: (f) Failure of adequate trial of first-line therapy (including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine).
criteria.text: Failure of an adequate trial of first-line therapy, including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine.

source_quotes.quote: MEDICAL NECESSITY: Advanced neuroimaging is considered medically necessary when at least ONE of the following red-flag criteria is documented in the medical record:
(a) Sudden-onset severe ("thunderclap") headache reaching maximum intensity within one minute.
(b) New or progressive focal neurologic deficit (motor, sensory, visual, speech) associated with the headache.
(c) Significant change in headache pattern, frequency, or severity from the member's established baseline.
(d) Headache in an immunocompromised host or in the setting of active malignancy.
(e) Papilledema or other signs of increased intracranial pressure.
(f) Failure of adequate trial of first-line therapy (including at least two preventive agents for chronic migraine).

A prior negative non-contrast CT does NOT preclude MRI when red-flag features are present, as CT has limited sensitivity for posterior fossa lesions, small vascular malformations, and early ischemic change.
